skill.name: paper-tweet-generator
skill.description: Generates a structured reading tweet from an academic paper (PDF, Word, or Text), highlighting specific product advantages. Use when the user wants to turn a document into a social media post or reading summary.
skill.summary: Extracts text from academic papers and generates structured social media summaries with background, results, and conclusions.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 371 static findings originate from the extracted_text.txt file, which contains the full text of a legitimate Cell journal research paper (DOI: 10.1016/j.cell.2024.11.025). The paper's zinc-finger protein sequences, scientific citation notations (C2H2, C4 motifs), and DOI references triggered security heuristics but are standard academic content. The skill's actual code performs only safe filesystem operations (PDF/DOCX/TXT reading and text output writing) with documented Python scripts. No credential access, network calls, or command injection vectors exist in the skill code.

medium_findings.title: File Write Operation in extract_to_file.py
medium_findings.description: Writes extracted PDF text to an output file. The script uses a hardcoded default input filename from a prior development session but accepts command-line arguments. The file write is intentional and legitimate for the skill's text extraction purpose.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: File write is expected and necessary for PDF text extraction. However, the hardcoded default filename suggests development-stage code that was not cleaned up.

medium_findings.title: File Write Operation in scripts/extract_text.py
medium_findings.description: Writes extracted text from PDF/DOCX/TXT files to a user-specified output path. The output path is provided via command-line argument, not hardcoded. This is a legitimate and expected operation for a document text extraction skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Intentional file write to user-specified output path. No credential or sensitive data exposure risk. Expected functionality for the skill purpose.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution via Python Scripts
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md documents shell commands to run Python scripts for PDF text extraction. Commands are hardcoded and use documented script paths. No user-injected arguments in the command strings themselves.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Commands are well-documented and use fixed script paths. The user-provided file path is passed as an argument which carries minor injection risk, but is standard practice for CLI tools.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Development Filename in extract_to_file.py
low_findings.description: Default PDF filename (PMID:39742810-PIIS0092867424013412.pdf) is hardcoded in the script. This was used during skill development but is replaced when command-line arguments are provided.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Explicitly visible hardcoded filename at line 28-29. Minor code quality issue, not a security risk.

low_findings.title: Extracted Paper Content in Repository
low_findings.description: The extracted_text.txt file contains the full text of a Cell journal research paper. All security flags (C2 keywords, weak crypto, SAM database, IP address, reconnaissance) originate from this file and are standard academic/scientific content: zinc-finger protein sequences, citation DOIs, and biochemical notations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: File content clearly matches a published Cell journal paper about zinc-binding cysteine proteome. All suspicious patterns are false positives from legitimate scientific terminology, protein sequences, and reference DOIs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Analysis Heuristic False Positive - No Credential Access
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged heuristic patterns for credential access based on combination of filesystem and network patterns, but no credential access, API keys, or secrets exist in any skill file. The heuristic was triggered by the combination of file write operations and URLs in the extracted paper content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of all 10 files confirms zero credential access. Heuristic was a false positive from the academic paper text mixing with extraction script operations.

content.user_title: Generate Reading Tweets from Academic Papers
content.value_statement: Researchers and science communicators spend hours reading and summarizing academic papers for social media. This skill extracts text from PDF, Word, or text files and generates a structured reading tweet with background, results, and conclusions.
actual_capabilities: Extracts text content from PDF, DOCX, and TXT files using Python scripts
Generates structured tweet sections including title, background, results, and conclusion
Highlights specific product or drug advantages mentioned in the research paper
Supports customizable prompt templates for different summary styles
Handles large documents by focusing on abstract and key sections
limitations: Cannot extract text from scanned PDF images (requires OCR preprocessing)
Requires Python environment with pypdf and python-docx packages installed
Output quality depends on the original paper's text extraction quality
Does not generate images, infographics, or multimedia content for tweets

use_cases.title: Pharmaceutical Research Communication
use_cases.description: A medical affairs professional extracts key findings from a clinical trial paper and creates a structured thread highlighting drug efficacy data for stakeholder communication.
use_cases.target_user: Medical affairs and pharmaceutical communications teams

use_cases.title: Academic Lab Social Media
use_cases.description: A graduate student generates a reading tweet summarizing their lab's newly published paper, including background context and key results, for sharing on academic Twitter.

use_cases.title: Science Journalism
use_cases.description: A science writer processes multiple research papers to extract core findings and generate consistent structured summaries for news articles or newsletter content.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Paper Summary Tweet
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user wanting a simple tweet from a paper
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a research paper about [TOPIC]. Extract the basic information, background, key results, and conclusion. Generate a structured reading tweet with a catchy title. Highlight the main discovery and its significance.

prompt_templates.title: Product-Focused Research Thread
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this paper about [DRUG/PRODUCT NAME]. Create a 4-part reading tweet thread: 1) Basic info and research background, 2) Study design and methods, 3) Key results highlighting [PRODUCT] advantages, 4) Conclusion and clinical significance. Ensure all drug terminology uses standardized naming.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Paper Literature Summary
prompt_templates.scenario: Comparing findings across multiple papers on the same topic
prompt_templates.prompt: I have extracted text from several papers about [TOPIC]. Compare the key findings across these papers. For each paper, generate: the title and journal, the main research question, the most significant result, and the conclusion. Then provide a cross-paper synthesis.

prompt_templates.title: Critical Review Style Tweet
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this research paper critically. In your tweet summary, include: 1) What the authors claim and what they actually show, 2) Strengths of the methodology, 3) Limitations or gaps not addressed, 4) How this advances the field. Be honest about overreach and underpowered claims. Do not fabricate findings.

output_examples.input: PDF file: Cell journal paper about the human zinc-binding cysteine proteome
output:
Paper Reading Thread: The Human Zinc-Binding Cysteine Proteome (ZnCPT)

1/4: A new study in Cell maps 6,173 zinc-binding cysteines across the human proteome, revealing how zinc regulates protein function at an unprecedented scale. This is the deepest analysis of zinc-regulated biology to date.

2/4: Background: Zinc is essential for many biological processes, but exactly which proteins it binds to was largely unknown. The team developed ZnCPT to systematically identify zinc-binding sites across thousands of proteins.

3/4: Key Results: ZnCPT identified 52 cancer genetic dependencies sensitive to zinc binding. The researchers discovered that zinc inhibits glutathione reductase (GSR), driving cell death in GSR-dependent lung cancers.

4/4: Conclusion: This resource provides a foundation for understanding zinc regulation in health and disease, with particular implications for cancer therapy. The data is publicly available as a community resource.

output_examples.input: Research paper about a new drug mechanism for diabetes treatment
output:
New in [Journal Name]: [Drug Name] Shows Promise in Metabolic Control

Background: Metabolic diseases affect millions worldwide. Current therapies have limited efficacy in certain patient populations.

Results: [Drug Name] demonstrated a 40% improvement in glucose control compared to standard therapy. The mechanism involves activation of [Pathway X] which was previously underexplored.

Conclusion: This study opens a new therapeutic avenue for metabolic disease management. Phase 2 trials are underway to confirm these findings in broader populations.

best_practices: Always verify the extracted text quality before generating the tweet, especially for PDF files with complex layouts or scanned images.
Review and edit the generated tweet sections to ensure claims match the original paper and no fabricated data is included.
For very long papers, focus the extraction on the abstract, introduction, results, and conclusion sections to stay within context limits.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill to fabricate research findings or make claims not supported by the source material.
Do not bypass text extraction and paste raw paper text directly into prompts expecting the skill to handle context overflow.
Do not publish generated tweets without verifying accuracy of drug names, dosages, statistical claims, and citations against the original paper.

faq.question: What file formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill supports PDF, DOCX (Word), and TXT files. For scanned PDFs that contain images instead of text, the extraction may return empty results.

faq.question: What Python packages are required to run the extraction scripts?
faq.answer: You need pypdf for PDF files and python-docx for Word documents. Install them with: pip install pypdf python-docx.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for papers in languages other than English?
faq.answer: The extraction scripts work with any language that has text in the document. However, the prompt templates work best with English text. Output quality may vary for non-English content.

faq.question: Does this skill access the internet or send data externally?
faq.answer: No. All processing happens locally on your machine. The skill reads files from your local filesystem and generates text output. No network requests are made.

faq.question: How long can the extracted text be?
faq.answer: Very long documents (over 50,000 characters) may exceed context limits. The workflow automatically focuses on abstract, introduction, results, and conclusion sections for large documents.

faq.question: Can I customize the tweet format or add different sections?
faq.answer: Yes. The prompt templates in references/prompt_templates.md can be edited to change section format, add new sections, or adjust the tone and style of the output.
